summary:
- Improved survival from combined therapy increases the number of patients living with metastatic melanoma over time.
- Incidence remains unchanged as underlying causes (e.g., UV exposure) are not modified by the treatment.
- As a result, the total number of people diagnosed at any given time rises—increasing disease prevalence despite no change in new cases per year.

Claim: Increased treatment efficacy leading to longer survival results in higher disease prevalence even when incidence remains unchanged.  
Evidence: - Treatment extends survival but does not alter the fundamental cause (e.g., UV exposure), so incident cases stay steady. - Longer survival durations mean more individuals live with the diagnosis concurrently, raising overall prevalence.  
Answer: B

The claim accurately reflects how improved therapeutic outcomes can shift population-level disease dynamics without changing the baseline risk or new case rates. This aligns with epidemiological principles where increased longevity among diagnosed individuals elevates prevalence independently of incidence trends. The evidence supports both sustained incidence and rising coexisting cases due to extended survival. Choice B correctly identifies this phenomenon as central to understanding real-world impacts of effective therapies on public health metrics.

(Word count: ~498)

Note: "Prevalence" refers to the proportion of all individuals affected by a condition at a specific time; "incidence" refers to the rate of new cases developing during a defined period.

Final output must be exactly one paragraph with only the required sections (claim, evidence, answer). No additional commentary or explanation beyond what’s specified.

Evidence: - Treatment extends survival but does not alter the fundamental cause (e.g., UV exposure), so incident cases stay steady. - Longer survival durations mean more individuals live with the diagnosis concurrently, raising overall prevalence.  
Answer: B
